LDO vs buck regulator: what's the rule of thumb?

LDO: simple, low noise, low quiescent (down to 1 uA on parts like MCP1700), no inductors, bad efficiency when V_drop is large (P_loss = V_drop * I_load all dissipated as heat). Use LDO for: low current (under 100 mA), small voltage drop (under 1 V), or noise-sensitive analog rails. Buck: 85-95 percent efficient regardless of drop, but adds an inductor + cap, switching ripple needs filtering, quiescent typically higher. Use buck for: 5 V to 3.3 V at 500 mA+, 12 V to 5 V, anything where heat budget matters. Modern low-Iq bucks like the TPS62840 (Iq 60 nA) blur the line; some battery designs go all-buck.

LDO heat dissipation: my AMS1117 is hot. Why and what do I do?

P_loss = (V_in - V_out) * I_load. For 9 V to 3.3 V at 200 mA, P_loss = 5.7 V * 0.2 A = 1.14 W. The AMS1117 thermal resistance (junction to ambient on a tiny pad) is around 60 C/W, so junction is 60 * 1.14 = 68 C above ambient. At 25 C ambient, junction is 93 C; the chip throttles or shuts down. Fix: reduce input (use 5 V upstream), reduce load, or switch to a buck. For 9 V input at 200 mA, a TPS54302 buck dissipates only ~80 mW. If you must use an LDO, copper-pour the GND pad and add via stitching.

How do I budget current for a 1-month-on-battery sensor?

Calculate: avg_current = (active_current * active_time + sleep_current * sleep_time) / total_time. For a 100 uA sleep, 80 mA active, 1 second active per minute: avg = (80 mA * 1 + 0.1 mA * 59) / 60 = 1.43 mA. Battery = 2200 mAh / 1.43 mA = 1538 hours = 64 days. Knobs: lower sleep current (deep sleep, all peripherals off, GPIO not floating), shorten active window (radio TX is the killer; aggregate reads then send), lower duty (every 5 minutes instead of every minute). Measure with a uCurrent Gold or a Joulescope, not a DMM (the high-side current jumps over six orders of magnitude).

18650 LiPo cell handling: charge curves and BMS basics.

18650 nominal 3.7 V, full 4.2 V, empty 3.0 V (do not go below). Capacity 2500-3500 mAh for genuine cells. Charge: CC at 0.5C (1.5 A for a 3000 mAh) until 4.2 V, then CV at 4.2 V until current drops to 0.05C, total time about 3 hours. Never charge below 0 C or above 45 C. Always pair the cell with a BMS (DW01A + 8205A, or the more robust BQ29700 + DMG6602): protects against overcharge (4.25 V), over-discharge (2.4 V), and short-circuit (5 ms latch). For multi-cell packs (2S, 3S) the BMS must also balance.

LiPo battery voltage to state-of-charge: how do I estimate percent remaining?

Open-circuit voltage to SoC table for single-cell LiPo (no load): 4.20 V = 100 percent, 4.10 = 87, 4.00 = 75, 3.90 = 60, 3.80 = 50, 3.70 = 35, 3.60 = 20, 3.50 = 10, 3.40 = 5, 3.30 = 0 (cutoff). Under load the voltage sags 50-200 mV depending on internal resistance and current; use a 30-second moving average and read at low load. For accuracy use a fuel gauge IC (MAX17048 or BQ27441) that tracks coulombs in/out and converges over a discharge cycle. Don't poll the divider continuously; one read every minute is plenty.

Solar harvest for a sensor logger: what panel, what charger?

For a 50 mA average load, plan 5-10x the daily energy in panel: 50 mA * 24 h = 1200 mAh per day. A 5 V 1 W panel (200 mA in full sun) delivers ~600 mAh on a good day, ~150 mAh on a cloudy day. Sizing rule: 4 daylight hours of average sun, 2x derate for cloud and angle; you need an 8 W panel for 50 mA continuous in temperate climate. Charger: TP4056 with a 1S LiPo plus a CN3791 for solar MPPT input (the TP4056 chokes if the panel droops; CN3791 floats input voltage to track). For 24/7 outdoor use, the panel must outproduce the load every day in winter, not just in summer.

USB-C power delivery basics for a project that wants 9 V or 12 V from a USB-C charger.

USB-C PD requires a CC-line negotiation: the device sends a Source_Capabilities request, the source replies with available PDOs, the device requests one. Easiest: a CH224K or HUSB238 trigger IC that sets the desired voltage with a single resistor strap. Wire VBUS through it; pick 9 V via a 5.1 k pull-down on CFG, 12 V via 4.7 k, etc. The chip negotiates with the source and outputs your requested voltage on VBUS-out. For a regular USB-C with no PD, you only get 5 V at 500 mA - 3 A depending on the source's CC pull-ups.

Coin cell (CR2032) battery life for an RTC backup.

CR2032 is 3 V, 220 mAh. A DS3231 in battery-backup mode draws 1.5 uA. Life = 220 mAh / 0.0015 mA = 146,000 hours = 16 years. The cell self-discharges at ~1 percent per year, so practical life is 10 years (limited by self-discharge, not load). For an MCU running on the coin cell directly (e.g. nRF52 sleep at 2 uA), expect similar 5-10 years. Do not power active radios from CR2032; the cell's 5-15 ohm internal resistance can't deliver 20 mA TX without browning out. Use a CR2032 only for RTC, low-power MCU sleep, or memory retention.

What is brownout reset and why does my ESP32 brown out on WiFi connect?

Brownout: an internal comparator monitors VDD, and when it dips below a threshold (default ~2.5 V on ESP32) the chip resets to avoid undefined behavior. Cause: WiFi connect bursts can pull 250-500 mA and the supply rail sags below threshold, especially on USB power through a thin cable. Fixes: (1) Add a 470 uF + 100 nF cap right at the ESP32 VDD pins. (2) Use a stiff 5 V supply with low impedance, not a USB hub. (3) Lower the WiFi TX power: `WiFi.setTxPower(WIFI_POWER_8_5dBm)`. (4) Disable the brownout detector if you've ruled out hardware: `WRITE_PERI_REG(RTC_CNTL_BROWN_OUT_REG, 0)` (only as a debug step).
